mechanism_narrative: A rapid increase in the unemployment rate signals a sharp deterioration in the labor market, a leading indicator of recession. This increases risk aversion and lowers corporate earnings expectations, driving negative equity returns over the subsequent quarter.
candidate_trigger: UNRATE increases by > 0.2pp in a single month
evidence_refs: fact:Unemployment rate is a key coincident/lagging indicator for NBER recession dating.
failure_condition: S&P 500 produces positive 60-day forward returns in more than 50% of triggered instances over a full business cycle.

mechanism_narrative: Sustained momentum in nominal retail sales indicates robust consumer demand, which translates into higher corporate revenues and earnings, supporting positive equity market returns over the next month.
evidence_refs: fact:Consumer spending constitutes approximately two-thirds of U.S. GDP.
failure_condition: The 3-month change in RSAFS fails to show a statistically significant positive correlation with 20-day forward returns over any 5-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: When the labor market is tight (low unemployment) and inflation is accelerating, the Federal Reserve is forced to tighten monetary policy. The anticipation of higher rates contracts valuation multiples and hurts equity prices.
candidate_trigger: UNRATE < 5.0% and 3-month annualized change in CPIAUCSL > 3.0%
evidence_refs: doc:Federal Reserve dual mandate on price stability and maximum employment.
failure_condition: The S&P 500 has positive 60-day returns in more than 50% of periods where the trigger condition is met.

mechanism_narrative: A significant contraction in housing starts signals a severe downturn in a key cyclical sector. This negative signal is amplified when monetary policy is not yet accommodative, indicating a policy constraint or error, leading to negative equity returns.
evidence_refs: fact:The housing market collapse began in 2006-2007, preceding the broader financial crisis.
failure_condition: The signal has no predictive power (Rank IC < 0.03) during periods where the 3-month change in FEDFUNDS is non-negative.

mechanism_narrative: Positive momentum in industrial production reflects an expanding supply side of the economy, correlating with strong corporate output and profitability, which drives positive short-term equity returns.
evidence_refs: fact:Industrial production is a core component of the Index of Coincident Economic Indicators.
failure_condition: The 3-month change in INDPRO shows a negative correlation with 20-day forward returns over a 3-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: A decisive cut in the Federal Funds Rate by the FOMC signals a shift to an accommodative monetary policy stance. This reduces borrowing costs for corporations and lowers the discount rate for future earnings, boosting equity valuations.
candidate_trigger: FEDFUNDS decreases by > 0.40pp in a single month
evidence_refs: fact:The FOMC cut rates by 50bps in Sep 2007 and 25bps in Oct 2007.
failure_condition: S&P 500 20-day forward returns are negative in more than 50% of instances following a >40bps rate cut.

mechanism_narrative: A concurrent slowdown in both job creation and industrial production indicates a broad-based weakening of the economy, affecting both household income and corporate output. This dual shock leads to lower earnings forecasts and negative market returns.
candidate_trigger: 3-month average change in PAYEMS < 50 and 3-month change in INDPRO is negative
evidence_refs: fact:PAYEMS growth was weakening through mid-to-late 2007.
failure_condition: The S&P 500 shows positive 60-day returns in over 40% of periods where both job growth and industrial production are weak.

mechanism_narrative: A surprisingly sharp monthly decline in housing starts indicates an abrupt contraction in the construction sector and broader economic sentiment, triggering a rapid, negative repricing of risk assets over a short horizon.
evidence_refs: fact:Housing data is a closely watched leading indicator, prone to causing short-term volatility.
failure_condition: The average 5-day forward return following a >10% monthly drop in HOUST is not statistically different from zero (t-test p > 0.10).
